question: A 27-year-old G1P0000 woman presents to her obstetrician’s office at 24 weeks gestation complaining of new hair growth on her upper lip. She now needs to shave her face every day or else thick hair appears. She also has worsening acne that started several weeks ago. Her pregnancy thus far has been complicated by gestational diabetes, which is managed by diet and exercise. She has a male fetus based on her 20 week anatomy ultrasound. The patient has a medical history of polycystic ovary syndrome. The patient’s temperature is 98.5°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 121/76 mmHg, pulse is 70/min, and respirations are 13/min. The cardiopulmonary exam is normal, and the patient’s abdomen has a fundal height of 31 cm. A pelvic exam reveals an unremarkable closed cervix and a right-sided adnexal mass without tenderness. Transvaginal ultrasound is performed and shown in Figure A. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Laparoscopy to prevent detrimental effects on the fetus
options.C: Percutaneous umbilical blood sampling to determine prognosis
options.E: Spironolactone to treat hirsutism and acne

raw_response:
Step 1: List key features from the case  
- 27-year-old woman, 24-week pregnancy  
- New facial hair ("hirsutism") starting weeks before  
- Acne exacerbation  
- Known prior diagnosis of polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) — classic cause for these symptoms during pregnancy  
- No complications noted except gestational diabetes (well-controlled with diet/exercise so far)
- Cardiopulmonary exam is normal; fundal height at 31 cm (which is about 7 weeks above expected for 28-week gestation but may be due to measurement error)
- Pelvic exam shows right-sided adnexal mass, no tender

Step2: Analyze the findings  
An adnexal mass found on exam suggests possible ovarian cyst/tumor. PCOS commonly causes multiple small ovarian cysts, especially if she’s hyperandrogenemic—could explain hirsutism and acne.

The question asks what is the *most appropriate next step* in management given this presentation.

Step3: Consider each option  
A. Laparoscopy to prevent detrimental effects on fetus – only relevant if there were signs of torsion/compression, but no such info here. Unnecessary intervention unless urgent.  
B. Laparoscopy to remove a malignancy - masses can be benign or malignant, but transvaginal ultrasound was done first per standard protocol. Mass is present but imaging done already. Definitive biopsy via laparoscopic or other means would follow after further assessment. Not yet.  
C. Percutaneous umbilical blood sampling (PUBS)? That’s used for fetal blood disorders (e.g., incompatibility), completely unrelated to maternal gynecologic findings described. Incorrect.  
D. Reassurance – likely inappropriate as there is a physical finding needing evaluation.  
E. Spironolactone to treat hirsutism and acne – this is a mainstay of hormonal therapy for PCOS-related hirsutism/acne even pre-pregnant. But she's pregnant. Spironolactone is teratogenic in early pregnancy and generally avoided until postpartum. However, in a clinical scenario like this, sometimes clinicians consider managing the underlying cause while monitoring closely. But with a detected mass, you don MCQ context we must prioritize diagnostic workup over immediate treatment unless it's emergent.

But let’s clarify: In pregnancy with suspected large ovarian cyst/masses related to PCOS and concerning symptoms/hirsut

question: A 19-year-old man presents to his primary care physician. He is asymptomatic and has no acute complaints, has no medical problems, and takes no medications. He was born in the United States but moved to El Salvador at age 11 and has only recently moved back to the United States in the past year. He was up to date on his vaccinations prior to his move to El Salvador. He last received a diphtheria, tetanus, and acellular pertussis (DTaP) vaccine 14 years ago at age 5, the second dose of a meningococcal vaccine 3 years ago, and an influenza vaccine 2 months ago. The patient’s temperature is 98.2°F (36.8°C), blood pressure is 120/76 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 16/min. There are no rashes noted on physical exam. Which of the following vaccine regimens is indicated in this patient?

options.C: Tetanus, diphtheria, acellular pertussis, and influenza

raw_response:
Step 1: Review the patient’s history.
– Born in US, lived abroad in El Salvador from age 11 until now (~9 years).
– Last Tdap (tetanus, diphtheria, acellular pertussis) before moving was as a child; over 10 years ago.
– Meningococcal vaccine given with the second dose ~6 years ago.

Step2: Consider recommended vaccination schedules for adults who may have missed childhood vaccines due to prolonged time away or lack of routine immunizations during adolescence.
– The standard adult Tdap booster is every 10 years after the third dose (which usually includes Td, DTP, and part of Tdap).
– Since it’s been >10 years since his last Tdap, he likely needs one.
– MenACWY (meningococcal) is recommended for all adolescents in many countries, including the US, starting at 11 through 12 years old. Given here as a 19-year-old without evidence of completion of the series (no mention of booster or first two doses post-childhood), and assuming possible incomplete previous vaccination per travel/high-risk exposure, the current recommendation favors catch-up with meningenococcal serogroup W/Y/VMenB (assuming that was what he got—commonly MenACYW-Yv or MenB depending on country protocol).

But among the options provided:
• B covers TdaP – overdue  
• A covers influenza – annual  
• C covers Tdap + flu + possibly others — though the phrasing isn’t clear if it’s specifically Tdap+flu, which would be optimal for someone returning from high-risk exposure  
• D = TdaP alone (since Td is combined into Tdap now); appropriate if Tdap is overdue  
• E doesn’t include pertussis, so less comprehensive

Step3: Assess risk factors relevant to the visit.
No new exposures reported, but significant gap in Tdap. As an adolescent living overseas for several years with unclear recency of previous Tdap, current guidelines recommend a Tdap booster within the past 10 years unless immune via earlier doses. So Tdap is needed.

Step4: Influenza should still be given annually regardless, even if other vaccines are overdue because flu can change seasons.

However, the question asks “Which of the following vaccine regimen IS INDICATED?” For a young adult like this with documented overdue Tdap AND needing a seasonal flu shot (he did get influenza just 2 months ago,

question: An 8-week-old boy presents with his mother to the pediatrician for a well visit. The patient has been breastfed since birth, and usually feeds for 30 minutes every 2-3 hours. The patient’s mother is concerned that her milk production is not keeping up with the patient’s nutritional requirements. She reports that about 2 weeks ago the patient began regurgitating breastmilk through his nose and mouth after some feeds. He seems mildly upset during the episodes of regurgitation but usually settles down quickly and is hungry again soon afterwards. His mother has already tried limiting the volume of each feed, which seems to have reduced the frequency of regurgitation. She denies any diarrhea, hematochezia, or family history of food allergies. Her older son had a similar problem with vomiting that resolved around 12 months of age. Four weeks ago, the patient’s height and weight were in the 40th and 34th percentiles, respectively. His height and weight are now respectively in the 37th and 36th percentiles. His temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 72/49 mmHg, pulse is 121/min, and respirations are 32/min. On physical exam, the patient is cooing in his mother’s lap and smiles reciprocally with her. He lifts his head and shoulders off the examination table when placed in the supine position. His abdomen is soft, non-tender and non-distended. Bowel sounds are normoactive. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
raw_response:
The scenario describes an 8-week-old infant who was previously well-fed via breastfeeding but started regurgitating breastmilk. This suggests possible lactose intolerance due to inadequate maternal lactation supply compared to demand—a condition known as **lactose intolerant reflux** from insufficient foremilk (colloid) feeding at frequent intervals. The baby’s growth is slightly decreasing (height -3rd percentile change; weight -2nd percentile change over ~4 weeks), prompting concern about nutritional adequacy.

Key points:
- Regurgitation of breastmilk after certain feeds.
- Mother can produce enough milk overall because the baby still wants to nurse frequently—likely mixed feeding (shorter nursing sessions, perhaps lying flat too early).
- No mention of structural issues like GERD or other symptoms requiring imaging or medication yet.
- Growth is only modestly delayed—not severely malnourished.
- There is no evidence of infection, hypoglycemia, or other red flags suggesting a metabolic or infectious cause needing an ultrasound or antibiotics.
  
Proton pump inhibitors (A): Unnecessary in infants; no acid-related concerns mentioned here.
Reassurance/counseling on positioning (C): Good supportive advice while waiting for further evaluation—but this alone won't address potential nutritional deficit if breastfeeding isn’t sufficient.
Switch to hydrolyzed formula (E): Hydrolyzed formula may help if there is **lactase deficiency**, allowing better digestion of lactose, possibly reducing reflux and improving tolerance. In cases of lactose intolerance/reflux linked to underfeeding (e.g., short/nasal-frequent spitting up/regurgitation despite adequate intake *overall*), switching to a hypoallergenic/hydrolyzed formula can improve symptoms and nutrition.
Obtain abdominal ultrasound (B): Only indicated if there is suspicion of congenital biliary disease or other intra-abdominal pathology—which is not suggested here based on the presentation.

Since the main issue appears to be poor transfer/digestion of nutrients from breastfeeding (possibly due to poor letdown, lying back too soon, or mild lactase deficiency), the best initial step before considering more invasive interventions would be to try adjusting the method of breastfeeding to optimize colloid intake (positioning the baby upright, increasing frequency/intensity of short, efficient nursing, etc.). However, given the clinical context commonly seen in underfeed situation leading to possible malnutrition and failure to thrive associated with lactose intolerance, **switching to hydrolyzed formula (E)** is often recommended as it breaks down lact

question: A 31-year-old man presents to the emergency department for fever, malaise, and cough. For the last week, his cough has been progressively worsening, and he has been noticing blood in his sputum. He denies any recent travel or sick contacts and infrequently smokes cigarettes. Approximately 5 weeks ago, he presented with similar symptoms and was found to have a right upper lobe lung infiltrate, a CD4 count of 40/mm^3, and an HIV viral load of 115,000 copies/mL. He was appropriately treated and discharged home. Four weeks after initiation of treatment, his CD4 count was over 400/mm^3 and HIV viral load was negligible. His temperature today is 102°F (38.9°C), blood pressure is 130/90 mmHg, pulse is 100/min, and respirations are 20/min. A radiograph of the chest demonstrates new nodules in the left upper lobe and hilar adenopathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Continue current anti-retroviral treatment, antimicrobial, and anti-tuberculoid therapies
options.B: Determine drug sensitivities against the patient’s pulmonary infection
options.E: Start high-dose glucocorticoid treatment and discontinue antiretroviral therapy

raw_response:
Step-by-step reasoning:

1. The patient is a **31-year-old male** with **progressive cough**, **hemoptysis (blood in sputum)**, newly appearing **nodules in the left upper lung lobes**, and **hilar lymphadenopathy**.
2. These findings suggest the development of **new pulmonary infections or malignancy**, possibly superimposed on an underlying **HIV/AIDS-related complication** due to prior history described.
3. Given the **rise in CD4 count and suppression of HIV viral load** after starting ART (antiretroviral therapy) five weeks ago, it indicates that the previous opportunistic infection may have been related to advanced immunosuppression that is now resolved with effective therapy.
4. However, the **current imaging shows new lesions**, which could represent:
   - A recurrence or ongoing infection despite improved immune status,
   - Progression of a previously undiagnosed lesion into a mass/carcinoma (e.g., non-AIDS-related lung cancer),
   - Or another process like tuberculosis reactivation, fungal infection, etc.—but without specific mention of these diseases.
5. The key concern here is identifying the cause of the **new pulmonary changes**: nodules and possible infection signs require further diagnostic work-up before changing treatments.
6. Option **A** suggests continuing all existing therapies but does *not* address the new abnormality detected. 
7. Option **B** (“Determine drug sensitivities”) refers specifically to testing antibiotic sensitivity if there’s an infectious etiology (like TB or pneumonia), but this would first require either a sputum culture, biopsy, or other diagnostics—none specified as the immediate next step based solely on available information.
8. Options **C** (discontinue antibiotics) or **D** (D-dimer, suggesting thromboembolic disease) lack strong evidence from the presentation alone; discontinuation of ART (option E) is dangerous given the unknown cause of the new lesions and could be life-threatening if the issue is an untreated infection (even though CD4/virral load look better).
9. The most prudent next step when seeing progressive new parenchymal changes in someone with a complex past (opportunistic illness controlled by ART) is to **investigate the causes of the current respiratory abnormalities** via further imaging studies (CT scan) or tissue/biopsy/extrapulmonary evaluation.

However, among the provided options, **the best approach is to continue monitoring and evaluate for persistent infection

question: A 57-year-old woman presents to the emergency department for a headache. She was at home when a sudden headache began with greater severity than she had experienced in the past. She presented within 30 minutes of symptom onset. The patient has a medical history of sinusitis, constipation, diabetes, and pelvic inflammatory disease. Her current medications include fexofenadine, oral contraceptive pills, and metformin. She is currently taking amoxicillin for a sinus infection. She smokes 1 pack of cigarettes per day and drinks 2-3 alcoholic beverages daily. Her temperature is 99.5°F (37.5°C), blood pressure is 187/118 mmHg, pulse is 120/min, respirations are 17/min, and oxygen saturation is 98% on room air. On physical exam, the patient is noted to be uncomfortable with the appearance shown in Figure A. Neurological exam reveals a confused woman who is now complaining of "seeing double." Cardiopulmonary exam reveals mild bibasilar crackles and tachycardia. Abdominal exam is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?
raw_response:
The patient presents with sudden-onset binocular vision loss (" seeing double") which suggests acute ischemic ocular event such as retinal artery blockage—most commonly carotid stenosis or embolism. Given her risk factors including hypertension from smoking, recent antibiotics (possible C. difficile colitis causing hypokalemia or other issues less likely here), and possible cardiovascular concern due to rapid symptoms and exam findings, but specifically the visual deficit pointing toward an urgent vascular problem in the brain.

On examination, confusion plus diplopia immediately raises suspicion for stroke, especially given her age, risk factors (hypertension, hyperlipidemia implied by history), and new neurological deficits. In emergent cases of possible stroke, **CT head** is first-line imaging because it rapidly evaluates for hemorrhagic vs ischemic changes and identifies large vessel occlusion.

Other options:
- A & E are antibiotics; she already started amoxicillin.
- D (MRI) is slower than CT and not first step unless CT is contraindicated or negative for bleed.
- Dexamethasone would be if there were inflammation, but no mention of eye pain or trauma.
So the best immediate next step is to rule out acute intracranial pathology urgently — that is **B. CT of the head**.

Answer: B

question: A 52-year-old G3P3 woman presents to clinic with a 2-year history of urinary incontinence. She has had frequent, involuntary loss of urine over the last 2 years but presented today because of 10 days of dysuria. She has been treated for 2 urinary tract infections over the past 6 months. She does not endorse any loss of urine with coughing or laughter. She has no chronic medical illnesses and no surgical history. Her temperature is 99.5°F (37.5°C), blood pressure is 120/80 mmHg, pulse is 92/min, and respirations are 15/min. Her BMI is 30 kg/m^2. On pelvic examination, the vagina is dry and atrophic with a 2-cm tender, palpable anterior vaginal mass. The mass does not change with Valsava maneuver. Her postvoid residual volume is 60 mL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
raw_response:
Step 1: Assess the patient’s symptoms.
- Urinary incontinence for 2 years, recent dysuria (10 days), prior UTIs (last 6 months).
- No incontinence with coughing or laughing → rules out stress incontinence.

Step 2: Physical exam findings.
- Tender, palpable anterior vaginal mass measuring 2 cm that doesn’t change with Valsalva maneuver → suggests a structural lesion rather than dynamic causes like prolapse during effort.
  
Step 3: Consider different options:
A. **Overflow incontinence**: Usually due to bladder outlet obstruction; classic presentation includes constant dribbling despite normal filling, often associated with increased PVR (postvoid residual) if there is retention. question: A 46-year-old woman presents to her primary care doctor to request a referral to ophthalmology. Her vision has been steadily declining over the past 2 years and she thinks she needs a prescription for glasses. She further complains that her lips and feet feel numb. Her medical history is notable for medullary thyroid cancer status post total thyroidectomy. Her periods are regular. She enjoys a diverse diet and takes levothyroxine. Her blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 80/min, and respirations are 12/min. She is alert and oriented. Her pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light, but appear opacified. Extraocular movements are intact, albeit slow. Her visual acuity is decreased bilaterally. The remainder of her physical exam is unremarkable. Her basic metabolic panel is shown below: Na+: 139 mEq/L Cl-: 100 mEq/L K+: 4.9 mEq/L HCO3-: 25 mEq/L BUN: 10 mg/dL Glucose: 110 mg/dL Creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL Thyroid-stimulating hormone: 1.5 µU/mL Ca2+: 7.0 mEq/L Phosphorus: 6.5 mEq/L Albumin: 3.6 mg/dL The patient's ECG is shown in Figure A. What is the most likely cause of this presentation?
raw_response:
The patient’s key features include:
- **Age**: 46-year-old female.
- **Medical history**: Status post **medullary thyroid carcinoma**, which commonly arises from parafibromatous calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) tumors—often part of multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2 (MEN2), particularly associated with **neuroendocrine differentiation** or parathyroid involvement if it was the parathyroid, though here the tumor is explicitly described as medullary (not papillary).
- **Symptoms**: Visual decline (“numbing” of lips and feet — classic early sign of **hypocalcemia**) and possible other subtle symptoms fitting a systemic process.
- **Lab findings**: 
    - **Low serum calcium (7.0 mEq/L; normal ~8.5–10.2)** → suggests hypocalcemia.
    - Other labs (Na+, K+, glucose, creatinine, albumin) are mostly within range except low calcium.
    - No evidence of hyperkalemia (K+ is only mildly elevated at 4.9 mEq/L, normative ranges vary more widely than for hypokalemia in adults).
    - TSH is normal (1.5 µIU/mL can be low-normal or mildly low depending on reference, so no clear hypothyroid state yet unless context implies overt hypothyroid from surgery—but levothyroxine use would usually prevent that.
    - No mention of hyperparathyroidism or bone pain, but the calcitonin-producing medullary thyroid cancer might coexist with parathyroid damage, especially during surgery (though rare, iatrogenic hypoparathyroid can occur).
- **Clinical signs**: Pupils are “opaque,” slow extraocular movements may reflect neuromuscular irritability due to hypocalcemia.

Now consider each option:

*A.* Hyperkalemia typically causes muscle weakness, EKG changes (peaked T waves, etc.), but her clinical focus is *visual* and *peripheral nerve* symptoms plus lab hints at low calcium—not high potassium. Unlikely.

*B.* Iatrogenic hypoparathyroidism occurs after surgical manipulation near or removing the parathyroids, leading to hypocalcemia—a common complication when treating thyroid cancers surgically because the nearby isthmus is often damaged. This fits perfectly given the surgical history and symptoms of numbness and visual changes from hypocalcemia.

*C.*

question: A 69-year-old man passes away after a 3-week hospitalization for liver failure, complicated by a myocardial infarction. A medical student rotating in the intensive care unit asked if she can practice performing intubations during her rotation. The resident suggests performing one on the deceased patient, as the student cannot harm him. Which of the following is the most appropriate course of action for the medical student?

options.A: Ask the resident if the patient’s family or the patient has been consented for this procedure
options.C: Contact the patient's family to ask for permission to perform the procedure
options.D: Intubate the patient with the resident's supervision
options.E: Report the resident to hospital administration

raw_response:
The scenario describes a situation where the medical student wants to practice intubation on a deceased patient without the possibility of causing harm because the patient cannot be harmed physically post-mortem. However, even in such cases, ethical and institutional protocols generally require **consent from next of kin** before performing invasive procedures on a deceased body—this is an important teaching moment about respect for the deceased and their family.

Option analysis:
- **A**: Asking about consent aligns with standard protocol regardless of the patient’s clinical status; it ensures that no rights or wishes are violated.
- **B**: Consulting the ethics committee may be overkill unless there is ambiguity about the procedure being allowed or inappropriate for educational purposes—but asking the resident (who would know these things) is more practical first.
- **C**: Contacting the family is reasonable since they represent the legal authority to make decisions regarding the decedent.
- **D**: Performing the intubation on a dead patient is unethical and potentially disrespectful; while technically possible due to lack of airway resistance, it should only ever be done under proper guidance but not without acknowledging the need for consent.
- **E**: Reporting the resident is unlikely to be justified here—the issue isn't misconduct, but process.

Best approach is either **A** or **C**, depending on local policy. But typically, the immediate responsible step would be to ask the attending/resident about whether practicing on a deceased patient is acceptable for education—it’s better to check through the supervising physician rather than bypass protocol entirely (**A**) or involve unnecessary parties like the full ethics committee (**B**) unless specifically required beyond basic consent considerations. Still, since the patient is already deceased, formal consent might not be legally necessary once death is confirmed, so directly proceeding (**D**) could be seen as unprofessional or disrespectful in some contexts, especially early in training.

Therefore, the **most appropriate initial action** is to seek direction/supervision via the resident or attending, who will explain that consent/permission is still ethically expected from the family, though legally redundant at this point. Among the options given, the best fit is:

**A. Ask the resident if the patient’s family or the patient has been consented for this procedure**

This shows professionalism and respects both institutional policy and cultural sensitivities around working with the deceased.

Final Answer:  
Answer: A

question: A 55-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband with a 1 hour history of an unremitting headache. The headache started suddenly while she was eating dinner and she says it feels like the “worst headache of my life.” An emergent CT scan of the head without contrast confirms the diagnosis, and a CT angiogram identifies the source of bleeding. The patient undergoes surgical management of her condition. On hospital day 3, she is found to be disoriented to person, place, and time. She also develops nausea and vomiting. Her medical problems consist of heart failure for which she takes furosemide, spironolactone, and metoprolol, which were continued at admission. Her temperature is 99.6°F (37.6°C), blood pressure is 100/60 mmHg, pulse is 112/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination shows poor skin turgor. Capillary refill time is 4 seconds. Serum laboratory results are shown below: Na+: 120 mEq/L Cl-: 92 mEq/L K+: 3.9 mEq/L HCO3-: 26 mEq/L BUN: 32 mg/dL Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL Serum osmolality is 265 mEq/L and urine osmolality is 340 mEq/L. Urine sodium is 44 mEq/L. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
raw_response:
The key features here include a 55-year-old woman with sudden severe headache, identified as intracranial hemorrhage via CT (likely aneurysm or arteriovenous malformation causing a subarachnoid or intracerebral bleed). Despite diuretic use (furosemide, spironolactone, metoprolol) leading to electrolyte disturbances—specifically **hyponatremia** (Na+ 120 mEq/L indicates low sodium due to water overload), but the critical findings now are neurological in acute presentation:

- Disorientation to person, place, and time suggests *cerebral edema*.
- Associated symptoms of nausea/vomiting further support brainstem irritation or increased ICP from hematomas.

Electrolytes show *hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis*, typical with excessive diuresis when potassium is normal (K+ 3.9). However, the main issue developing after surgery would be **brain injury-induced cerebral vasodilation**, shifting the extracellular fluid into the intracranial space — classic sign of **intracranial hypertension secondary to a space-occupying lesion such as a subarachnoid hemorrhage or massive intracerebral hemorrhage**, both often associated with elevated ICP requiring rapid intervention if untreated. 

Now consider the options:
A. Addison disease – unrelated to current presentation.
B. Cerebral salt wasting – rare, usually post-surgery but more common in chronic illnesses; causes *hypovolemic* state with *low* plasma Na+, not high.
C. Diuretic overuse – can cause hyponatremia and dehydration signs (but not this level of confusion unless extreme case).
D. Primary polydipsia – excessive thirst, but no direct link to these new neuro symptoms.
E. Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone (SIADH)—causes hypernatremia (high sodium), opposite of what we see here (her sodium is actually low).

But let’s double-check: In subarachnoid or intracerebral hemisphere bleed, there may be **cerebral swelling/increased ICP**, sometimes complicated by **post-traumatic hypoxemia, edema, or shifts in body fluids**. However, the primary complication that matches the mental status described (**confusion/disorientation**) shortly after a craniotomy/surgical manipulation (for a bleed) is **intraoperative or post-operative cerebral ed

question: A 14-year-old boy presents to his pediatrician with a 2-month history of intermittent arm and leg spasms. He says that the spasms usually occur after he wakes up as he is getting ready for school, and consist of disorganized jerking motions that stop after a few minutes. He has also noticed that they occur when he stays up late to cram last minute for exams. He has not noticed any other symptoms. He has no medical history and takes no medications. He recently started experimenting with alcohol and marijuana that one of his friends has been stealing from their parents. His temperature is 98.6°F (37.0°C), blood pressure is 115/70 mmHg, pulse is 70/min, and respirations are 12/min. Physical exam reveals a healthy child. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?
raw_response:
The key features described are intermittent arm and leg spasms triggered by sleep deprivation or staying up late, occurring mostly upon waking and during periods of stress related to studying. These spasms resolve spontaneously without neurological decline.

Analyzing each option:

A. **Absence seizures**: Typically involve brief, sudden lack of awareness/drowsiness ("absent" movements) rather than visible jerk-like muscle contractions; more common in younger children but often accompanied by other signs like drop attacks or postictal confusion if generalized.

B. **Alcohol intoxication**: Acute ingestion can cause transient ataxia, hypotension, and generalized tonic-clonic or myoclonic movements, especially if recent binge drinking occurred. However, these are usually more prominent with active drinking, and the scenario here emphasizes experimentation—not chronic abuse—though possible acute effects could mimic the presentation.

C. **Cannabinoid use**: THC affects brain regions involved in motor control and timing of seizure initiation (e.g., cortico-cerebellar and thalamo-cortical interactions). Intermittent, stress-triggered jerks are reported with recreational cannabis, particularly in teens. The onset pattern aligns better with withdrawal/stress-related myoclonus from cannabis than absence seizures.

D. **Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (JME)**: Characteristic of adolescent-onset JME is nocturnal myoclonic jerks, often upon awakening or after sleep deprivation—but they tend to be repetitive over time, not strictly discrete episodes tied to specific triggers like staying awake late. No mention of day-time events or focal versus generalized spasms.

E. **West syndrome** (infantile spasm): Early childhood condition involving three types of seizures (tonic, clonic, absent), typically short-lived but less linked to study/sleep patterns and generally outgrown by adolescence.

Best fit among them:
- The description matches stress-induced myoclonus.
- Among options given, both B and C relate to substance use, but *cannabis* is known to provoke isolated, stress-sensitive myoclonic twitching without full convulsions.
- Acute intoxication might explain some movement changes, but the classic association in adolescents presenting intermittently after sleep loss is more strongly linked to **CBD or THC effects**, specifically increased likelihood of myoclonic jerks due to altered GABA/A2a receptor modulation and corticolimbic activation (especially THC).
- Thus, while “acute intoxication” may produce odd movements,

question: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department after he developed pain in his right leg after landing off a ski jump. The patient is otherwise healthy and does not take any medications. He states his pain is 10/10. He is requesting medications and is crying out in pain. His temperature is 98.7°F (37.1°C), blood pressure is 149/85 mmHg, pulse is 103/min, respirations are 15/min, and oxygen saturation is 99% on room air. Physical exam reveals swelling over his right leg and knee. The patient cries out in pain with passive motion of the foot at the ankle. A radiograph is performed as seen in Figure A. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
raw_response:
The presentation includes severe pain localized to the right knee (crying out when the foot is moved passively at the ankle suggests involvement just proximal to it) with swelling and inability to bear weight post-sudden trauma—a common pattern for both patellar fractures (from direct blow or displacement during the injury) and posterior knee dislocations (often associated with high-impact activities like skiing jumps that can cause hyperextension or lateral translation of the tibia). However:

- **Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tears** typically present with medial joint lock, instability, and meniscal signs; there’s no mention of locking here.
- **Apophysitis of the tibial tubercle** usually affects children due to repeated friction from boots and has a different clinical picture than this acute traumatic event.
- **Tibial plateau fracture** would involve pain and possible mechanical deficit but less commonly causes immediate severe knee pain with passive movement of the distal segment unless accompanied by other injuries.
- **Patellar fracture**: Common in high impact on the kneecap. Pain is often localized to the front of the knee, especially if displaced. Passive movements involving the lower part near the patella could be consistent. This is a classic trauma scenario—skiers can land awkwardly causing a direct injury to the patella. On X-ray, a patellar fracture appears as a bicondylar break.
- **Posterior dislocation of the knee** involves the femur and tibia rotating behind the gluteus maximus. It may cause severe pain and an inability to move the knee far, including issues with the foot position depending on reduction status. But in many cases, the focus is more on the back of the knee, and the description emphasizes pain with movement of the *foot* (distal end), which doesn’t fit well.

Passive movement of the *foot* at the ankle implies the knee injury extends into the affected area, making ACL or PCL injuries less likely without specific findings. Given the mechanism (landing hard on the knee), a **patellar fracture** is highly probable because the patella is vulnerable to such forces. While posterior knee dislocation might affect movement, the specificity of the pain location ("knee," not specifically mentioning the back or locking) and the fact that the patella is directly anteriorly displaced makes **C. Patellar fracture** the best initial choice based on history alone and the described symptoms. 

Final Answer:
\boxed{C}

question: A 31-year-old woman presents to her obstetrician for her first prenatal visit after having a positive home pregnancy test 1 week ago. Her last menstrual period was 8 weeks ago. The patient has a medical history of type 1 diabetes mellitus since childhood and her home medications include insulin. Her hemoglobin A1c 2 weeks ago was 13.7%. At that time, she was also found to have microalbuminuria on routine urinalysis. Her primary care provider prescribed lisinopril but the patient has not yet started taking it. The patient’s brother is autistic but her family history is otherwise unremarkable. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37.0°C), blood pressure is 124/81 mmHg, pulse is 75/min, and respirations are 14/min. A physical exam is unremarkable. This fetus is most likely at increased risk for which of the following complications?
raw_response:
The patient is 31 weeks pregnant based on the timeline given ("last menstrual period 8 weeks ago," gestational age ≈ LMP + 38 weeks = ~46 weeks; however, with a positive home test 1 week before this estimate, it still suggests around 28–30 weeks, but we focus on risks related to her comorbidities rather than gestational age alone here).

Key facts:
- **Type 1 diabetes mellitus** during pregnancy increases risk for **gestational diabetes**, neonatal hypoglycemia if poorly controlled, macrosomia, and other metabolic issues.
- **Microalbuminuria** indicates early kidney dysfunction, raising risk for preeclampsia, fetal growth restriction, and potentially other maternal and perinatal complications.

Now evaluate each option:

A. **Aneuploidy**:  
This can occur due to advanced maternal age (>35) or certain chronic conditions like diabetes, though typically the main risk factor cited for aneuploidy is older age. However, there’s some evidence that poor glycemic control in gestational or pre-existing T1DM may increase the risk slightly—not as strongly established as for neural tube defects from pre-conception glucose exposure.

B. **Neonatal hyperglycemia**:  
Yes—both mother's prior T1DM and current possible poor control raise the risk of high birth sugar levels at birth, leading to hyperglycemia.

C. **Neural tube defect (NTD)**:  
NTDs such as spina bifida mainly relate to maternal folate deficiency *before* conception through early pregnancy. Since this diagnosis came recently and no mention of dietary factors, NTD isn’t directly linked unless there were undiagnosed metabolic issues earlier—but standard teaching ties NTD to folic acid status.

D. **Oligohydramnios**:  
Not clearly supported by the info provided about diabetes/microalbuminuria.

E. **Post-term delivery**:  
No reason indicated—the estimated due date would be around 46 weeks; her symptoms don't suggest post term.

question: A 51-year-old man presents to the emergency department complaining of fever and a cough with yellow-green sputum for the past 3 days. He denies any hemoptysis, facial pain, rhinorrhea, or night sweats. The patient reported similar symptoms 2 months ago. Chest radiograph performed at the time was notable for a right middle lobe consolidation. He was diagnosed with lobar pneumonia and completed a 7-day course of antibiotics with complete resolution of his symptoms. He has no other significant medical history and does not take any medications. He has smoked 1 pack of cigarettes a day for 35 years and drinks 3 beers per week. He is sexually active with his wife. His temperature is 101.7°F (38.7°C), blood pressure is 127/85, pulse is 102/min, and respirations are 22/min. Physical exam is notable for decreased breath sounds on the right side without wheezing and increased dullness to percussion on the right side. A chest radiograph is ordered and reveals a consolidation in the right middle lobe. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: CT of the chest without contrast

raw_response:
The patient is a 51-year-old male with a new right-sided consolidation after having previously had a similar infection that resolved. Current vital signs suggest ongoing illness (elevated temp, tachycardia), but there is no mention of risk factors such as immunocompromise or atypical pneumonia clues. On physical exam, he still has decreased breath sound on the right—possible residual disease—or progression.

Reviewing the options:

A. **Barium swallow**: Used to evaluate esophageal strictures or motility; not indicated here.
B. **CT of the chest without contrast**: A high-resolution CT can help characterize the lesion (lobar vs. infiltrative, abscess formation, etc.), assess airway obstruction, or look for complications like empyema. Given this presentation and prior lobar pneumonia resolving partially, imaging may be useful if there’s concern about persistent/increasing opacity, pleural effusion, or atypical findings now.
C. **HIV antibody assay**: Only reasonable if HIV exposure is suspected (recent STI, intravenous drug use, unexplained lymphadenopathy, etc.). No such exposures are mentioned.
D. **Interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA)**: For tuberculosis (TB) diagnosis—not relevant given the current clinical context.
E. **Serum immunoglobulin levels**: These might be considered in suspected systemic infections (like TB, viral infections causing immune deficiency), autoimmune diseases, or malnutrition—but again, no evidence these are likely causes based on history provided.

Key considerations:
– Right middle lobe consolidation suggests bacterial cause, possibly re-infection or complications (such as **empyema**, which sometimes follows pneumonia).
– Persistent or worsening respiratory changes merit further evaluation before more aggressive treatments.
– Since he was treated for pneumonia earlier but still has some right-side findings, the best next step would be to rule out **complication** such as an abscess or extension (which a chest CT can do better than plain film).
  
Plain CXR may show little, so the most appropriate advanced imaging to detect complications is **CT scan of the chest**, especially without contrast because it offers higher resolution for detecting subtle changes compared to repeat x-ray. 

Thus, the answer is:

Answer: B

question: A 1-year-old girl is brought to a neurologist due to increasing seizure frequency over the past 2 months. She recently underwent a neurology evaluation which revealed hypsarrhythmia on electroencephalography (EEG) with a mix of slow waves, multifocal spikes, and asynchrony. Her parents have noticed the patient occasionally stiffens and spreads her arms at home. She was born at 38-weeks gestational age without complications. She has no other medical problems. Her medications consist of lamotrigine and valproic acid. Her temperature is 98.3°F (36.8°C), blood pressure is 90/75 mmHg, pulse is 94/min, and respirations are 22/min. Physical exam reveals innumerable hypopigmented macules on the skin and an irregularly shaped, thickened, and elevated plaque on the lower back. Which of the following is most strongly associated with this patient's condition?
raw_response:
The key features described in the case point toward a bone or CNS neoplasm rather than a cardiovascular or ocular disorder.

**Analysis of symptoms and findings:**
- **Hypsarrhythmia on EEG:** This indicates abnormal electrical activity in the brain, often seen in children, particularly those with certain types of brain tumors or developmental disorders affecting the cerebral cortex.
- **Seizures starting at 1 year old after increasing over two months:** Seizure onset in early childhood can be linked to intracranial lesions such as tumors or dysplasias.
- **Inumeral hypopigmented macules on skin:** Hypopigmentation suggests loss of melanin, which is classically seen in *neurofibromatosis* caused by **Neurofibromatosis Type 1 (NF1)**—this presents with café au lait spots—and more relevant here, NF1 is associated with **ependymal tumors**, especially **penduncular astrocytomas (also called ependymoma)** that may present with seizures and possible neurological deficits. However, among the options given:
    - **Cardiac rhabdomyoma (A):** Most common cardiac tumor in infants; typically non-progressive and resolves spontaneously by adolescence but does not explain the EEG pattern or skin findings.
    - **Glaucoma (B):** Unlikely etiology for these EEG and skin signs in a young child.
    - **Optic glioma (C):** Tumors near the optic nerve can cause vision changes and sometimes seizures, but the classic presentation with hypopigmented patches is less typical unless part of a syndrome like NF1.
    - **Polyostotic fibrous dysplasia (D):** A disorder involving abnormal bone development leading to multiple bony lesions (hypoplasia/fibro-sclerotic lesions); also, rare forms of fibrous dysplasia can affect the central nervous system, presenting with seizures, cortical abnormalities, and, less commonly, cutaneous manifestations if there’s vascular involvement (though not classic).
    - **Renal cell carcinoma (E):** Highly unlikely in a 1-year-old; kidneys tumors are very uncommon before puberty except for congenital anomalies.

However, the combination of **seizures, hypsarrhythmia, skin hypopigmentation**, and the mention of recent neurologic workup strongly evokes a genetic disorder affecting both bone/membrane structures and neural tissue. While **optic gli

question: A 25-year-old woman presents for a new patient physical exam. Aside from occasional shin splints, she has a relatively unremarkable medical history. She takes oral contraceptive pills and a multivitamin daily. All of her age appropriate immunizations are up to date. Her periods have been regular, occurring once every 28 to 30 days with normal flow. She is sexually active with 2 partners, who use condoms routinely. Her prior pap smear was performed last year and was normal. Her mother has diabetes and coronary artery disease, and her father passed away at age 45 after being diagnosed with colon cancer at age 40. Her grand-aunt underwent bilateral mastectomies after being diagnosed with breast cancer at age 60. Her temperature is 36.7°C (98°F), blood pressure is 115/80 mmHg, pulse is 65/min, respirations are 12/min, and oxygen saturation is 100% on room air. Her physical exam is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation for this patient?

options.E: Pap smear with human papillomavirus (HPV) DNA testing now

raw_response:
The patient is 25 years old. Key points:

- **Family history**: Father died young from colon cancer (diagnosed at 40); grandmother had early-age (age 60) breast cancer and subsequent mammography because of early diagnosis.
- This suggests value of early screening for both colon and breast cancers.

**Current guidelines**
- For women aged 21–29, **colorectal cancer screening starts at age 45**, but some sources recommend starting at 40 based on family history. However, standard USPSTF recommends *beginning* at age 50 without family risk; with significant family history (parental or very early paternal cancer), screenings may be started earlier—such as ages 35+, but there’s no routine screening before 45 per many current protocols unless high-risk features suggest otherwise.
- **Colonoscopy** can start at 45, sometimes sooner if polyps found earlier in relatives.
- **Breast cancer screening:** Mammography for average-risk women typically begins at age 40, though the general guideline is 45–53 with annual mammograms. Given the grandmother had surgery due to breast cancer, it supports early mammography for the daughter even if slightly older than usual.
  
But looking strictly at what’s “most appropriate” for this specific 25-year-old:
- There is NO standard recommended colorectal cancer screening yet (too early).
    - Option A (“colonoscopy in 5 years”) might align with waiting until 30 (if using 35–44 as first screen range, then starting at 40 would mean next opportunity is in ~5 years—but that seems late).
    - Option B says wait 10 years—which is much too delayed.
- For **cervical cancer screening,** the standard is Pap smear every 3 years beginning at age 21. Some guidelines consider starting at 25 acceptable since she’s 25 now—and she already had a recent normal Pap. So another one in 5 years (option D) could be reasonable, maintaining frequency rather than timing. But HPV + conventional PAP together at co-test interval isn’t indicated here unless high-risk factors exist.
- The presence of a male ancestor dying young from colon cancer emphasizes familial concern for colon issues, but she’s still too young for typical screening.
- **Mammography now (C)** for average-risk, per current recommendations would generally NOT be advised—it should wait till 40 or so.
- **Pap smear now (with or without HPV, depending on country

question: A 4-day-old neonate is brought to the pediatrician with vaginal discharge for the last 2 days. Her daughter has been feeding and voiding well. The neonate was delivered at 39 weeks gestation via an uncomplicated vaginal delivery and was discharged home after 2 days. The prenatal course was complicated by chlamydia in the mother during the first trimester, for which she and the partner were both treated with a confirmatory test of cure. The biological father is no longer involved in the patient's life, but her mother’s boyfriend has been caring for the baby whenever the mother rests. The neonate’s temperature is 98.5°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 56/35 mmHg, pulse is 138/min, and respirations are 51/min. She appears comfortable, and cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are unremarkable. There are no bruises or marks on her skin. Examination of the genitals reveals no vulvar irritation or skin changes but there is scant pink mucoid discharge at the introitus. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Warm water irrigation of the vagina

raw_response:
The scenario describes a 4-day-old infant with minimal symptoms—scanty pink mucoid vaginal discharge—but otherwise normal vital signs and no evidence of distress or complications from birth. Given that the discharge could be amniotic fluid (commonly present early postpartum) or possibly represent a low-grade infection such as vaginitis secondary to the mother’s recent exposure (even though treatment was given prenatally for chlamydial infection in the mother, but the newborn may still be exposed if the father is now the caregiver and sexual activity occurred recently).

Key considerations:
- **Amniotic fluid**: Normal for the first few days; does not require intervention unless excessive.
- **Vaginal discharge in a neonate**: Could be due to bacterial vaginosis, gonococcal/cervical chlamydial infection (less likely here since the mother was treated before delivery and the baby seems asymptomatic except for mild discharge).
- Chlamydia in pregnancy usually causes no immediate neonatal disease because antibiotics used during labor are often effective against chlamydia, especially if delivered vaginally and the mother received appropriate therapy.

The options analyzed:

**A. Reassurance:**  
This is reasonable initially because the clinical picture so far is benign and non-severe. No red flags for sepsis or significant abnormality detected yet.

**B. Report to child protective services:**  
No indication of abuse or neglect related to the case presentation. Unlikely without more concerning findings.

**C. Vaginal culture:**  
While cultures can assess for pathogens like *C. trachomatis* or other bacteria, they are typically reserved when there are higher-risk factors (e.g., prolonged rupture of membranes, fever, lethargic state, or suspicious for other infections). With minimal symptoms, this might be overinvestigative.

**D. Vaginal exam under anesthesia:**  
Unnecessary in this context. The infant is stable, showing no pain, and the finding is non-acute. Routine assessment would not justify sedation or invasive evaluation at this stage.

**E. Warm water irrigation of the vagina:**  
Not routinely recommended for minor, non-exudative, non-purulent discharge unless it is suspected to contain infectious material requiring dilution (such as pus or green/yellow discharge suggesting chlamydial lower genital tract infection [LGTI]). However, standard practice is cautious with irrigation unless clearly indicated for certain infections like herpes or less commonly, some STIs presenting with exudates

question: A 23-year-old woman presents to the emergency department for evaluation of fever and chills over the last several days along with a progressive rash and diarrhea. She had nasal septoplasty performed 1 week prior and has had nasal packing in place since that time. Her rash has progressed and the skin on her hands is "peeling." She has no significant medical history and takes no medications. Her temperature is 102°F (38.9°C), blood pressure is 84/54 mmHg, pulse is 160/min, respirations are 22/min, and oxygen saturation is 99% on room air. Exam reveals an ill-appearing woman. A diffuse, macular, erythematous rash is noted with desquamation over the patient's hands as shown in Figure A. Bilateral conjunctivitis is noted. The patient's nasal packing is removed revealing copious, foul-smelling, purulent discharge. Which of the following pathogens is likely responsible for this patient's condition?
raw_response:
The clinical presentation includes high fever, chills, purulent nasal discharge after sinus surgery, diffuse rash with peeling (desquamation) involving the hands, and bilateral conjunctival inflammation (conjugunctivitis/papilledema can be seen in some bacterial infections).

Key points:
- Recent nasal surgery (endoscopic procedures like septoplasty can introduce bacteria or disrupt local defenses).
- Purulent nasal discharge suggests a bacterial infection.
- Desquamation (skin peeling) especially on extremities, combined with systemic symptoms, may indicate a severe infectious process—could involve either viral (e.g., exanthem) or bacterial causes (cellulitis from sinusitis).
- Conjunctival involvement increases likelihood of community-acquired infection rather than something isolated to the nose.

Analyzing the options:

A. **Borrelia burgdorferi** — causative agent of Lyme disease; classic signs include erythema migrans and later neurological/radical symptoms, but not typical for this scenario.
  
B. **Neisseria meningitidis** — causes meningococcemia, with petechial-rash-to-warm-face-trunk involved, and sepsis. Meningococcemia often involves mucosal membranes (nose, mouth, throat) and can cause petechiae/dark pigmentation under the eyes, but less commonly starts with just the nose unless there was trauma. However, this symptom set could fit a meningococcal infection if it's disseminated.
  
C. **Rhinovirus** — common cause of upper respiratory tract illness, usually mild with runny nose and maybe a cough—but does not typically cause purulence, conjunctivitis, or extensive skin desquamation without other organ involvement.

D. **Rickettsia rickettsii** — Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever presenting with rash, fever, and sometimes mucosal involvement, but also involves the heart, liver, etc.; less directly linked to post-nasal surgery context here compared to others.

E. **Staphylococcus aureus** — particularly *S. aureus* can cause surgical site infections leading to cellulitis with rash extending beyond the sinuses/nasal area. Although she underwent nasal surgery, the rapidly progressing multisystemic symptoms (fever, conjunctivitis, mucositis) suggest a more invasive pathogen spreading via hematogenous routes (like bloodstream from a breakdown at the surgical site). question: A 4-day-old girl presents with her parents to the pediatrician for a routine visit. She was feeding well until this morning when she had several episodes of vomiting. The parents describe the vomitus as “bright green” and the patient has shown little interest in feeding since then. She was born at 36 weeks gestation to a 37-year-old G2P2 mother. The pregnancy was complicated by gestational diabetes, and all prenatal testing was unremarkable. The patient has not yet regained her birth weight and is in the 46th percentile for height and the 36th percentile for weight. The patient’s temperature is 99.2°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 68/46 mmHg, pulse is 132/min, and respirations are 32/min. On physical exam, the patient is in mild distress. She has no dysmorphic features. Her abdomen is distended and firm without guarding. Bowel sounds are hypoactive. The patient’s abdominal radiograph can be seen in Figure A and the patient’s upper gastrointestinal series can be seen in Figure B. Which of the following is the most likely etiology of this patient’s condition?
raw_response:
The key points from the case:

- **4-day-old infant**
- **Vomiting with bright green color** — classic sign of bile regurgitation, usually due to gastric outlet obstruction blocking passage of food back up into the esophagus.
- **Distended but non-tender abdomen**, no guarding → suggests non-perforated, mechanical obstruction rather than an infectious or inflammatory process causing peritonitis.
- **Hypoactive bowel sounds**: Indicates decreased intestinal motility which often accompanies bowel obstruction because the intestine isn't being pushed effectively past the blockage.
- **No significant maternal complications during pregnancy except gestational diabetes:** Unlikely to directly cause congenital biliary or gut defects, so the issue is probably anatomical.
- **Not yet returned to birth weight & low for age in growth charts:** Severe malnutrition possibly due to inability to pass feeds.

Also associated with high mortality due to delay in diagnosis (usually requires surgery within 48 hours).
- **Hirschsprung disease (B):** Causes chronic constipation, not acute vomiting. Usually lower GI tract (posterior wall of the intestines missing ganglia); doesn’t explain the vomiting or the appearance here.
- **Intestinal malrotation with volvulus (C):** Acute-on-acute presentation fits. Vomiting may occur if there’s a pre-volvular duodenum or proximal small bowel involvement. Small bowels may initially appear distended before becoming necrotic/hypotense later. Hypoactive bowel sounds possible due to partial obstruction/inflammation affecting motility. This is the classic scenario for bright green (bile-stained) vomiting plus failure to feed/malabsorption signs—especially in a newborn or young infant.
- **Jejunal atresia (D):** Less common. Can present similarly but jejunal obstruction typically causes more prolonged vomiting, diarrhea after resolution if perforation occurs, and specific clinical findings (e.g., distal gut obstruction symptoms). Not strongly supported here.

- **Intestinal malrotation with volvulus (C):** Acute-on-acute presentation fits. Vomiting may occur if there’s a pre-volvular duodenum or proximal small bowel involvement. Small bowels may initially appear distended before becoming necrotic/hypotense later. Hypoactive bowel sounds possible due to partial obstruction/inflammation affecting motility. This is the classic scenario for bright green (bile-stained) vomiting plus failure to feed/malabsorption signs—especially in a newborn or young infant.
- **Jejunal atresia (D):** Less common. Can present similarly but jejunal obstruction typically causes more prolonged vomiting, diarrhea after resolution if perforation occurs, and specific clinical findings (e.g., distal gut obstruction symptoms). Not strongly supported here.

question: A 55-year-old man presents to the emergency department with chest pain and shortness of breath. He has a medical history of hypertension, diabetes, and obesity. His temperature is 98.6°F (37.0°C), blood pressure is 177/118 mmHg, pulse is 127/min, respirations are 11/min, and oxygen saturation is 98% on room air. An ECG is performed and notable for ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. The patient is treated appropriately and transferred to the medical floor. On the 2nd day of his hospitalization, the patient has abdominal pain. His serum lipase is 272 U/L and his creatinine is 1.6 mg/dL. A physical exam is notable for the finding in Figure A. He is requesting pain medication for his abdominal pain. Which of the following is the most likely underlying diagnosis?
raw_response:
The initial presentation features ST-elevation in lead(s) II, III, and aVF, which suggests an inferior myocardial infarction given the clinical context—this pattern is typical for STEMI involving the posterior wall of the left ventricle due to occlusion of the left anterior descending (LAD) artery at the crux or distal diagonal branches causing ischemia to the inferior wall via circumflex artery.

The patient was already having a heart event managed as appropriate before developing abdominal pain. Serum lipase is elevated (>140 U/L), a common marker for pancreatic inflammation such as pancreatitis. While he had a prior MI, acute pancreatitis would be an unexpected new finding unless there were risk factors like gallstones (not mentioned here), alcohol use (also not mentioned), trauma, or severe hypertriglyceridemia. However, among the listed options, the development of **abdominal pain with elevated lipase** strongly points toward **pancreatitis**, even if the cause remains unclear. 

Other options:
- *Cholesterol embolism* typically causes sudden vascular symptoms but less commonly associated with persistent elevated lipase.
- *Heart failure and reinfarction* does not explain abdominal pain; reinfarction could worsen cardiac symptoms rather than create this new complaint.
- *Medication side effects* might rarely cause abdominal pain and no direct laboratory support here.
- *Renal failure* usually arises from pre-existing issues like AKI after another event, but lipase isn't specifically marked in that scenario.

Given the key clue (**elevated lipase + abdominal pain**) and the need for a process explaining both, **D. Pancreatitis** is the best supported answer despite the ongoing cardiac issue being present previously. Perhaps biventricular stress or comorbidities increased his risk for both events, making pancreatitis plausible—even if multifactorial.

Final Answer:  
Answer: D

question: A 55-year-old man presents to the emergency department with nausea and vomiting for the past week. He decided to come in when his symptoms worsened. He feels that his symptoms are exacerbated with large fatty meals and when he drinks alcohol. His wife recently returned from a cruise with symptoms of vomiting and diarrhea. The patient has a medical history of poorly managed diabetes, constipation, anxiety, dyslipidemia, and hypertension. His temperature is 99.5°F (37.5°C), blood pressure is 197/128 mmHg, pulse is 100/min, respirations are 17/min, and oxygen saturation is 95% on room air. Physical exam reveals a systolic murmur heard loudest along the left upper sternal border. Abdominal exam reveals an obese, tympanitic and distended abdomen with a 3 cm scar in the right lower quadrant. Vascular exam reveals weak pulses in the lower extremities. Which of the following is the most likely underlying etiology of this patient's symptoms?
raw_response:
Step 1: Let’s break down the key features presented about the patient.

Patient details:
• 55-year-old male  
• Nausea and vomiting for a WEEK (not acute or short-term)  
• Symptoms worse after large **fatty meals** and **alcohol**  
• Recent travel with **vomiting and diarrhea** (“dysenterial” symptoms possible post-cruise)  
• History includes poor glycemic control (diabetes), constipation, anxiety, high cholesterol & BP issues  
• On examination: **systolic murmur**, *tympanic* and *distended* abdomen, **weak pulses in lower extremities**, obesity

Now let’s analyze each symptom and sign:

1. **Nausea/vomiting**: Multiple causes – GI pathology, medication side effects, infections.
2. **Worse with fatty/alcoholic foods:** Classic for malabsorption disorders such as celiac disease or pancreatic insufficiency/diabetic pancreatitis.
3. **Recent travel + GI symptoms:** Suggests food-borne illness; norovirus is common but other pathogens also possible.
4. **Tympanic/distendedabd:** Can be seen in peritoneal irritation, ascites, or bowel obstruction.
5. **Weak peripheral pulses / hypotension / tachycardia:** May indicate volume depletion – could be due to chronic fluid loss (ascites), recent vomiting, or reduced cardiac output.
6. **Obesity (parasitic to BMI):** Not directly related unless complication of underlying condition.
7. **Systolic murmur:** Could be innocent, but in this presentation — especially given abdominal findings — suggests mitral valve prolapse, but more importantly, if there was a structural heart lesion causing shunting or increased volume, it might relate indirectly.
8. **Constipation vs current symptoms:** Current complaint is vomiting/diarrhea-like issue → less likely caused by ongoing constipation problems now.
9. **Impacted stool:** Unlikely to cause prolonged nausea and new-onset feverishness or systemic signs over one week without mention of pain or inability to pass gas/bowel movement.
10. **Adhesions/twisting (E):** Possible surgical scar in RLQ plus weak pulses suggest prior surgery (like gastrectomy, cholecystectomy, etc.), leading to bowel adhesions. But does twisting alone cause weeks-long nausea/vomiting?
11. **Norovirus (D):** Acute infectious

question: A 37-year-old man presents to the emergency department for a persistent fever. The patient has felt unwell for the past week and has felt subjectively febrile. The patient has a medical history of a suicide attempt and alcohol abuse. He is not currently taking any medications. The patient admits to using heroin and cocaine and drinking 5-8 alcoholic drinks per day. His temperature is 103°F (39.4°C), blood pressure is 92/59 mmHg, pulse is 110/min, respirations are 20/min, and oxygen saturation is 96% on room air. Cardiopulmonary exam is notable for a systolic murmur heard best along the left sternal border. Dermatologic exam reveals scarring in the antecubital fossa. IV fluids are administered and the blood pressure is subsequently 120/70 mmHg. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for the most likely underlying diagnosis?
raw_response:
The patient is a young adult with recent substance use, signs of sepsis (persistent high fever, tachycardia, hypotension that improved after fluid resuscitation), and physical findings including a new systolic murmur and skin scars (possibly from injection sites). These features strongly suggest intravenous drug use leading to **infected IV lines (catheters or needles)** causing bacteremia and possibly endocarditis.

**Stepwise analysis of options:**

A. **Blood cultures**: Essential first-line investigation when suspecting septicemia due to contaminated IV drugs. This would help identify an infectious cause requiring targeted therapy.
B. Chest radiograph: May show cardiac changes but does not directly diagnose the source of infection or assess for endocarditis specifically at this stage unless there’s suspicion of pulmonary complications—less immediate than starting antibiotics if sepsis is present.
C. CT scan: Useful for evaluating for complications like abscesses or valvular disease, but only after blood cultures are obtained given acute sepsis concern.
D. Ultrasound: For evaluting heart structures; useful if endocarditis is suspected once sepsis is confirmed, but not the initial diagnostic test here.
E. Vancomycin and gentamicin: Antibiotics should NOT be started before confirming the causative organism via blood cultures (risk of ophthalmic toxicity in GBS cases).

Given the presentation—fever plus risk factors for IV drug-related sepsis—the very next step should be obtaining blood cultures to detect potential bacterial pathogens. Starting antibiotics without confirmation can lead to overtreatment or harm (e.g., vancomycin ophthalmology issues). Other tests come later depending on culture results.

Thus:
Answer: A

question:
A 29-year-old pregnant woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 28 weeks of gestation, attends a routine prenatal visit. She denies vaginal bleeding, cramping, fluid loss, or reduced fetal movement. Initial prenatal labs showed an A-negative blood type, mild anemia, and positive IgG antibodies to Rhesus D, rubella, and varicella, with negative syphilis, hepatitis B, and HIV screens. Her first pregnancy was complicated by chronic placental abruption. Today, her vital signs are normal, and examination reveals a gravid abdomen with a fundal height of 26 cm. Laboratory results show an anti-D antibody titer of 1:32. Doppler velocimetry demonstrates peak blood flow velocity through the middle cerebral artery (MCA) at two times the median, with normal flow in the umbilical arteries and ductus venosus. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
Answer Choices: (A) Serial MCA Doppler studies and anticipated delivery at 37 weeks EGA (B) Amniocentesis for fetal lung maturity (C) Immediate cesarean delivery (D) Percutaneous umbilical blood sampling (E) Expectant management with weekly non-stress tests (F) Administration of anti-D immunoglobulin (G) Fetal steroids and delivery within 48 hours (H) Serial umbilical artery Doppler studies (I) Intrauterine fetal blood transfusion (J) Fetal steroids and delivery at 32 to 34 weeks EGA

options.A: Serial MCA Doppler studies and anticipated delivery at 37 weeks EGA
options.E: Expectant management with weekly non-stress tests
options.G: Fetal steroids and delivery within 48 hours
options.J: Fetal steroids and delivery at 32 to 34 weeks EGA

raw_response:
The patient is Rh-negative (A-) and has produced anti-D antibodies due to her previous Rh-positive pregnancy complicated by chronic placental abruption—this is a hemolytic risk scenario. Current findings include no acute complications but persistent anti-D antibodies detected via lab. The baby’s growth appears adequate based on the fundal height (26cm at 28wks ≈ near term), though the exact estimated gestational age (EGA) isn’t given numerically here.

- **Anti-D antibodies**: To prevent potential Rh isoimmunization and Hemolytic disease in future newborns, postpartum management includes prophylactic Rhogam (RhoGAM) administration.
    - However, during this active labor/late preterm appointment (~37 weeks), immediate intervention may be considered if there is evidence of fetal distress or other red flags from the ultrasound/doppler—but such are absent per current data except for prior abruption (which usually requires RhoGAM anyway).
- **Doppler studies** (options A/H): MCA peak velocities being twice the median could suggest early accelerations of labor but aren't necessarily urgent unless fetal compromise is found; otherwise, standard antenatal care applies.
- **Amniocentesis (B)**: Only indicated if uncertain about fetal lung maturity (before 35–36 wks)—not needed now.
- **Immediate C-section (C)**: No indications like cord prolapse, significant decelerations, or fetal malformation seen.
- **PUS (D)**: Unlikely as it’s reserved for evaluation of suspected fetal anomalies when standard ultrasounds are inconclusive.
- **Expectant management with NSTs (E)**: Possible but risky since she is likely to deliver soon regardless.
- **Fetal steroids (F/G/J)**: Indicated for lungs only if delivered before 30 wkg (Gestational Age <32 wk); not typically used at 28–32 wkg unless confirmed preterm birth imminent.
- **Serial Uterine Artery Doppler (H)**: Used to assess fetal well-being in high-risk pregnancies (like preeclampsia/unstable fetus—not applicable here).

question:
A 59-year-old female presents to clinic with a three-month history of urinary urgency, vaginal itching, and approximately four episodes of urinary incontinence daily, unrelated to coughing or sneezing. She experienced menopause at age 52 and reports pain during intercourse that has led to sexual abstinence with her husband. Her medical history includes a chlamydial infection in her twenties and three urinary tract infections in the past year. She takes no medications. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination reveals pale, atrophic vulvar skin with decreased elasticity and petechiae. The vaginal mucosa shows loss of rugae with areas of friable irritation and minimal clear discharge. Urinalysis is normal, fecal occult blood is negative, and pelvic ultrasound shows a small, retroverted uterus. What is the most appropriate initial management step?
Answer Choices: (A) Topical corticosteroids (B) Behavioral bladder training (C) Systemic antibiotics (D) Topical estrogen (E) Pelvic floor physical therapy (F) Oral selective estrogen receptor modulators (G) Vaginal moisturizers (H) Oral estrogen therapy (I) Anticholinergic medications (J) Nitrofurantoin
raw_response:
The patient is a postmenopausal woman with symptoms suggestive of **vaginitis**—specifically, genitourinary symptoms like urinary urgency, irritative vaginitis (vaginal irritation and scant discharge), and a history consistent with incomplete resolution after prior chlamydia treatment but possibly residual or recurrent lower genital infection due to immunosuppression from ages (reduced estrogen) leading to atrophy, increased susceptibility to infection, and genital inflammation. Her current exam findings support an inflammatory process such as **recurrent bacterial vaginosis**, though chlamydia was already treated. However, given the chronicity since menopause and the prominent genitourinary and vaginal findings despite previous UTIs being managed, the issue may be related to **vaginal atrophy secondary to decreased estrogen**, which causes dryness and microtrauma making the lining more susceptible to irritation/infection.

The best first-step management for **vaginal and urinary symptoms caused by atrophic vaginitis** typically involves restoring local estrogen therapy, either topical (D) or vaginal (not listed here; closest among options is **topical** if available) because this addresses the underlying cause (estrogen deficiency → atrophy/vaginal irritation).

Now evaluating the choices:
- **(A) Topical corticosteroid:** Used for inflammation/irritation, but not primary for atrophy/dryness without evidence of infection.
- **(B) Behavioral bladder training:** For urge incontinence only—not directly addressing the vaginal irritation or hormonal change.
- **(C) Systemic antibiotics:** No active infection detected now; inappropriate unless there’s culture results suggesting ongoing reinfection (which aren't provided).
- **(D) Topical estrogen:** Directly treats atrophic changes causing irritation/susceptibility to discomfort.
- **(E) Pelvic floor PT:** Only indicated if continence dysfunction remains unaddressed.
- **(F) Oral SRAs:** Also possible, but slower onset than topical.
- **(G) Vaginal moisturizer:** Good adjunct but less effective alone compared to estrogen for atrophic vaginitis.
- **(H) Oral estrogen therapy:** Effective but often started via topical/vaginal route first if possible, especially in elderly.
- **(I) Anticholinergics:** Not recommended initially as she likely has urge incontinence plus irritation.
- **(J) Nitrofurantoin:** Broad-spectrum antibiotic—if there were suspected bacterial vaginitis

question:
A 7-year-old female is admitted to the pediatric ICU following three days of abdominal discomfort and general malaise. Prior abdominal CT scanning at the referring facility showed no abnormalities despite suspected appendicitis.

The patient's ICU assessment reveals:
- Heart rate: 165 beats/minute
- Blood pressure: 74/32 mm Hg
- Oxygen saturation: 90% on room air
- Physical findings: Cool extremities, delayed capillary refill (6 seconds)
- Clear lung sounds
- Normal heart sounds without murmurs
- ECG showing sinus tachycardia
- Chest X-ray revealing enlarged cardiac silhouette

Which of the following medications should be administered immediately?
Answer Choices: (A) Dopamine (B) Furosemide (C) Milrinone (D) Epinephrine (E) Norepinephrine (F) Vasopressin (G) Phenylephrine (H) Dobutamine (I) Hydrocortisone (J) Amiodarone
raw_response:
Step-by-step reasoning:

1. **Clinical Presentation**: The child has abdominal symptoms suggestive of peritonitis but failed imaging, so there may still be concern for intra-abdominal bleeding or another acute process causing hypotension.
2. **Vitals**:
   - High HR (sinus tachycardia): Can be seen with sepsis, shock, pain, anxiety.
   - Low BP (74/32 mm Hg): Indicates significant hypovolemia or cardiovascular compromise—commonly shock from intra-abdominal hemorrhage even if appendix was negative on CT.
   - Low O₂ sat (on RA): Hypoxemia suggests poor tissue perfusion, likely due to systemic hypovolemia rather than primary pulmonary pathology (the EKG shows sinus tachycardia; CXR only shows an enlarged cardiac silhouette which can be artifactually seen in children).
3. **Other Findings**: Cool extremities, delayed refills — signs of poor peripheral perfusion consistent with distributive or low-output shock.
4. **Medications Needed Immediately**:
    - In severe hypotensive, tachycardiac critically ill pediatric patient, the priorities are reversal of hypovolemia, correction of shock, prevention of further decompensation.
    - **Fluid resuscitation first**, then vasopressors if unresponsive/resistant to fluids.
5. **Analysis of Options**:
    - **(A) Dopamine:** Used as a first-line inotropic support when myocardial depression isn't clear, or after fluid resuscitation in cardiogenic shock. Possible but not always immediate unless already failing to respond to fluids.
    - **(B) Furosemide:** Diuretic—not indicated acutely here.
    - **(C) Milrinone:** Inhaled bronchodilator/cardiuitary stimulant used mainly for refractory cardiac failure or certain arrhythmias—not typically first-line in shock.
    - **(D) Epinephrine:** For anaphylaxis or septic/shock patients who are unresponsive to fluid and/or vasopressors; commonly considered early vasopressor in septic/potentially hemorrhagic illness before definitive IV access if she’s already unstable.
    - **(E) Norepinephrine:** First-line vasopressor in shock (especially septic or distributive), especially when BP remains low despite other measures—intra-peritoneal bleeding often leads to distributive shock managed with

question:
An 84-year-old woman with a 5-year history of Alzheimer disease is brought in by her family for evaluation of worsening symptoms, including memory loss, wandering, and a decline in her ability to perform daily activities. She has been on donepezil for the past year, which initially provided noticeable improvement in her symptoms. However, her family is now uncertain if the medication is still benefiting her.

If the patient and her family wish to continue pharmacologic treatment, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in her drug therapy?
Answer Choices: (A) Add selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) to donepezil (B) Start antipsychotic medication alongside donepezil (C) Increase donepezil dosage (D) Add rivastigmine to donepezil (E) Continue donepezil (F) Discontinue all medications and start cognitive behavioral therapy (G) Replace donepezil with memantine (H) Replace donepezil with a non-pharmacological intervention (I) Switch to combination memantine/donepezil therapy (J) Discontinue donepezil without adding a replacement

options.A: Add selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) to donepezil
options.F: Discontinue all medications and start cognitive behavioral therapy
options.H: Replace donepezil with a non-pharmacological intervention
options.J: Discontinue donepezil without adding a replacement

raw_response:
Donepezil (galantamine) is an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor used as first-line pharmacotherapy for mild-to-moderate Alzheimer’s disease. Standard clinical guidance recommends continuing cholinesterase inhibitors like donepezil unless there are specific adverse effects or lack of tolerability, in which case alternatives may be considered under close supervision.

Reviewing the options:
- A and D suggest adding another agent; rivastigmine can sometimes be used adjunctively but isn't typically added after a positive response to donepezil.
- B introduces antipsychotics, which are generally reserved for symptom management such as behavior changes unresponsive to cholinergic therapy—not routine continuation.
